FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study longitudinal data; n=543 participants with complete data at year 2 (ages 11-13) and year 4 (ages 13-15) follow-ups

FT_IC2.evidence: Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (6-item, McDonald's ω=0.90) and Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (6-item, McDonald's ω=0.86) measuring specific addictive use behaviors

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed models with VGA × Time interactions on neural loss sensitivity: left nucleus accumbens (b=0.106, p=.002), left insula (b=0.103, p=.002), right insula (b=0.111, p=.001), right ACC (b=0.111, p=.001)

FT_IC4.evidence: Beta coefficients with 95% CI reported: VGA × Time interactions (b=0.106-0.111, CI [0.040, 0.180]); p-values (p=.001-.002); neural loss sensitivity × Time on VGA (right amygdala: b=0.086, p=.003; right ACC: b=0.088, p=.003)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis with fMRI Monetary Incentive Delay task; examined VGA/SMA associations with neural processing of rewards and losses across two time points

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article; Computers in Human Behavior, 165 (2025), pp. 108554; DOI: 10.1016/j.chb.2025.108554

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply. Paper reports bidirectional VGA ↔ neural loss sensitivity associations (VGA predicts neural changes AND neural loss sensitivity predicts VGA); also includes individual psychological predictor (loss sensitivity, not demographics-only). SMA analyzed cross-sectionally with own coefficients reported.
notes: Bidirectional relationship between VGA and loss sensitivity; SMA showed no significant long-term effects on reward/loss sensitivity. Study focused on neural mechanisms of behavioral addiction using fMRI.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Release 5.1; cross-sectional networks at T2 (n=9,054, ages 11-12 years) and T4 (n=4,007, ages 13-14 years); longitudinal CLPN analysis (n=3,954)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures problematic screen use (functional impairment via Video Game Addiction Questionnaire, Social Media Addiction Questionnaire, Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire) and screen time with specific breakdown: video games, social media, smartphones, TV, phone-checking frequency

FT_IC3.evidence: Network analysis with centrality measures (strength, expected influence) compared across networks; cross-lagged panel network (CLPN) models with autoregressive coefficients and cross-lagged effects predicting development; Benjamini-Hochberg correction applied

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric values reported: centrality indices (strength, expected influence with bootstrapped 95% CI); bridge strength differences between problematic use and behavioral tendencies (e.g., T2 Female: 0.28 vs. 0.12, 95% CI [0.054, 0.279]); correlation coefficients (e.g., r=0.47, p<0.001)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional network analysis at two time points plus longitudinal cross-lagged panel network analysis examining bidirectional relationships between screen use and developmental outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article; Journal of Behavioral Addictions, 14(2), pp. 724-737 (2025); DOI: 10.1556/2006.2025.00035

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply. Paper reports problematic screen use as both predictor and outcome in longitudinal models; includes individual psychological/behavioral predictors (behavioral tendencies, psychopathology, impulsiveness) beyond demographics; cross-lagged associations beyond demographics-only.
notes: Network analysis approach examining centrality and connectedness of problematic screen use. Problematic use showed greater centrality than screen time alone, with stronger associations to behavioral tendencies and psychopathology. Sex-specific networks analyzed separately.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data; subsample of 3,687 early adolescents (49.2% female, mean age 11.96 years) at year 2 follow-up with Fitbit sleep data; year 3 follow-up with mature screen usage questions

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures mature-rated video games (video game use frequency) and R-rated movies (watching frequency) as specific screen content exposure; assessed via questions: 'How often do you play mature-rated video games' and 'How often do you watch R-rated movies' with response options

FT_IC3.evidence: Prospective associations tested via structural equation modeling (SEM); reports t-tests and Johnson-Neyman analyses; sleep duration/bedtime/variability/social jetlag associated with mature gaming/movie watching 1 year later; interaction effects tested (sensation-seeking × sleep duration)

FT_IC4.evidence: Beta coefficients reported: mature video gaming (β=0.06, p<.001, 95% CI [-0.09, -0.02]), R-rated movies (β=-0.07, p<.001, 95% CI [-0.10, -0.03]); bedtime variability and social jetlag associations with p-values and confidence intervals throughout

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using ABCD data; secondary analysis examining prospective associations between sleep (measured via Fitbit wearables) and sensation-seeking at year 2 with mature screen usage outcomes at year 3

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article; Sleep, 48(1), pp. 1-12 (2025); DOI: 10.1093/sleep/zsae234; online publication October 11, 2024

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply. Paper reports individual psychological/behavioral predictors (sensation-seeking traits, sleep health characteristics) predicting mature screen usage; sensation-seeking is individual psychological characteristic, not demographic-only predictor. Bidirectional sleep-screen associations also reported.
notes: Prospective study examining sleep, sensation-seeking, and mature content consumption. Found 41.8% reported playing mature video games and 49% watched R-rated movies. Shorter sleep duration, later bedtimes, greater bedtime variability, and social jetlag associated with more mature screen usage 1 year later.
